coredata.dc:title: Orange carbon dot nanomaterial as optical/visual sensing platforms for morin and a biomass booster for plant seedlings

coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zhenhua
Yang, Xintong
Zhang, Quanxi
Zheng, Xianyun
Zhang, Yuexia
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: The precise regulation of morin levels in both diet and medicine is essential to evaluate the nutritional quality of food. Furthermore, plant yield is attracting considerable attention in the agricultural and herbal industries. Accordingly, the sensing platforms based on orange S and N co-doped carbon dots (SNCDs) were developed to detect morin through photoluminescence signals in aqueous solutions, solid matrices, and zebrafish. These sensing platforms exhibited excellent selectivity toward morin and possessed good anti-interference abilities, achieving limits of detection with 0.31 and 0.19 μM in the aqueous solution and solid state, respectively. Furthermore, the application of the as-prepared SNCDs at low concentration enhanced plant growth (using soybean seedlings as a model). The biological effects may be attributed to the promotion of light reaction and excess light reaction-induced injury. These findings offer novel insights into potential applications of SNCDs in sustainable agriculture and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensing platform
Morin
Plants
Biomass booster

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of morin in water solution and real samples
Bioimaging in zebrafish for detecting morin
Morphology, structure and composition of SNCDs
Photoluminescence properties and sensing platform for morin
Determination of morin in bovine serum
Effect of SNCDs on soybean growth

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of the SNCDs. (b) TEM image of SNCDs in water (inset: HRTEM image). (c) Size distribution. (d) FTIR spectrum. (e) Full XPS spectrum. (f–i) High resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and S 2p spectra of the as-prepared SNCDs.

(a) UV–vis and excitation/emission spectra of the SNCDs solution. The inset shows the fluorescence delay profile. (b) Tauc plot of the SNCDs. (c) The deconvoluted emission spectra of the as-prepared SNCDs. (d) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of the SNCDs. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of the SNCDs at various excitation wavelengths. (f) Emission spectra of the SNCDs in various solvents.

Selective and sensitive responses of the SNCDs to morin. (a) and (b) Selectivity of morin by the SNCDs toward morin against 58 potential interferences. (c) Fluorescence response in the presence of morin along with various antibiotics and amino acids. (d) Effect of morin on fluorescence intensity of the SNCDs. (e) Relationship between the fluorescence emission of SNCDs and morin concentration. (f) Two linear curves between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the SNCDs and morin concentration.
Photographs, CIE diagrams, and fitted plots of the prepared SNCDs for smartphone-assisted detection of morin under 365 nm (a–c) and 254 nm (d–f) light using the ColorPicker application.

(a) Photographs of the SNCDs/filter sensor for morin detection under 365 (top) and 254 nm (bottom). Fitted plots under 365 nm (b) and 254 nm (c). (d) Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of zebrafish incubated with SNCDs in the presence and absence of morin in bright field, dark field, and superimposed images.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of morin, SNCDs, and SNCDs-morin solution. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of morin, excitation, and emission spectra of the SNCDs. (c) Delay curves of the SNCDs before and after morin addition. The Inset plot shows the relationship between SNCDs lifetime and morin concentration. (d) Modified Stern−Volver plot. (e) Differential pulse voltammetry plots of the SNCDs and SNCDs-morin mixture. (f) Illustration of the Eg, HOMO, and LUMO of SNCDs and morin.

(a) and (b) Root and stem length, (c) fresh weight, (d) moisture level, (e) Total protein content, (f) soluble sugar, (g) NADPH, (h) NADP+, (i) ATPase Na+K+, (j) ATPase Ca2+, (k) ATPase Ca2+Mg2+, and (l) ATPase Mg2+ contents of the soybean seedlings treated with different concentrations of SNCDs. * p< 0.05, ** p< 0.01, *** p< 0.001 vs. the control group.
Detection of morin in bovine serum using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Orange carbon dot nanomaterial as optical/visual sensing platforms for morin and a biomass booster for plant seedlings

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Wucheng Road 92#, Taiyuan, Shanxi Province 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The precise regulation of morin levels in both diet and medicine is essential to evaluate the nutritional quality of food. Furthermore, plant yield is attracting considerable attention in the agricultural and herbal industries. Accordingly, the sensing platforms based on orange S and N co-doped carbon dots (SNCDs) were developed to detect morin through photoluminescence signals in aqueous solutions, solid matrices, and zebrafish. These sensing platforms exhibited excellent selectivity toward morin and possessed good anti-interference abilities, achieving limits of detection with 0.31 and 0.19 μM in the aqueous solution and solid state, respectively. Furthermore, the application of the as-prepared SNCDs at low concentration enhanced plant growth (using soybean seedlings as a model). The biological effects may be attributed to the promotion of light reaction and excess light reaction-induced injury. These findings offer novel insights into potential applications of SNCDs in sustainable agriculture and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Morin, a member of ﬂavanol class, can be obtained from various plants such as fruits, herbs, cereals, and seeds [1]. Currently, morin is extensively used in traditional Chinese medicine and food industries due to its exceptional properties, including antioxidant, antidiabetic, anti-inflammatory, antitumor, antibacterial, and neuroprotective effects, realized by modulating the activity of various enzymes [1–4]. Morin can serve as a beneficial agent in many dietary supplements and pharmaceutical preparations to inhibit the progression of many diseases [5]. However, excessive morin could exert some adverse effects such as gastrointestinal discomfort and liver damage gastrointestinal discomfort, liver damage [6]. Therefore, the detection of morin in biological systems is of great importance for human health [1,5,7]. Furthermore, the increased demand for flavanols is urgent and challenging. The increase in plant biomass can promote the development of agriculture, thereby ensuring sustainability and productivity [8,9]. However, plant production relies on the excessive use of pesticides and fertilizers, which can disrupt ecological system, resulting in issues such as reduced biodiversity and compromised food security [10,11]. The European Green Deal aims to reduce the use of pesticides by 50 % by 2030 [12]. Therefore, eco-friendly strategies to promote plant growth and to precisely regulate morin levels in both diet and medicine are urgently needed.

Carbon nanomaterials have emerged as eco-friendly materials for sensing morin and promoting plant crop yield, individually [3,13–16]. Several strategies based on carbon dots (CDs) have been developed for morin detection. Li et al. synthesized blue-emitting CDs using citric acid and polyethyleneimine to detect morin [17]. Unfortunately, the poor limit of detection (LOD) does not meet the demand for environment monitoring. Duan et al. prepared blue fluorescence CDs and constructed a nanocomposite of the CDs and polyethyleneimine, which could detect morin [3]. Despite much progress in morin detection, the short wavelength of the CDs limits their application in plant growth. Furthermore, orange fluorescence CDs from Nile blue sulfate and citric acid were developed as sensors to detect morin [18]. However, achieving a high quantum yield at long wavelengths does not necessarily result in a good LOD of the sensor. Moreover, some studies have demonstrated that metal-doped CDs [19,20] ultimately cause environmental pollution and pose a threat to the ecosystem. Therefore, it is necessary to synthesize long-wavelength CDs via a facile method and develop an accurate and reliable assay to precisely monitor morin level.

Furthermore, owing to biocompatibility and broad light absorption, carbon-based nanomaterials possess excellent physicochemical properties to promote plant growth [21–24]. Among these nanomaterials, CDs show great potential in converting light energy to chemical energy during the process of photosynthesis [13,22,25,26].

Nevertheless, CDs that can simultaneously detect morin and promote plant growth are rare [27,28]. Considering the advantages of CDs, long-wavelength CDs that exhibit broad absorption in the UV and visible regions may simultaneously meet the requirement for plant growth and morin sensing. Unlike conventional quantum dots, CDs are not significantly affected by the quantum confinement effect [17,29], which impedes the synthesis of CDs with long-wavelength fluorescence. In this study, we successfully fabricated S and N co-doped CDs (SNCDs) via a facile hydrothermal method with orange emission using safranine and sulfanilic acid as precursors ( Fig. 1a). Because of their excellent stability, SNCDs have great potential for practical applications. Furthermore, the sensing platforms of SNCDs were developed for morin detection through a combined quenching mechanism. In addition, the soybean was chosen as a plant model because the soybean plant is a typical dicotyledon and is easily obtainable and affordable. The prepared SNCDs promoted plant growth at low dose when soybean was chosen as the model.

All chemicals are listed in the Supporting information. SNCDs were synthesized from safranine and sulfanilic acid using a one-step hydrothermal method. In this process, 0.025 g safranine and 0.025 g sulfanilic acid were dissolved into 20 mL of ultrapure water following ultrasonic treatment. This solution was then transferred into an 80 mL Teflon reactor and maintained at 200℃ for 8 h in an air oven. Subsequently, the SNCDs solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min to remove any deposits. After filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, the supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1/4 500–1000) for 72 h. Finally, the resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain the SNCDs powder. The SNCDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy (see Supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of morin in water solution and real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, different concentrations of morin were mixed with the SNCDs solution, and fluorescence emission/excitation spectra were recorded. For the selectivity test, potential coexisting substances such as antibiotics, amino acids, biomolecules, anions, and cations in real samples were added to the sensing platform instead of morin, and the test procedure was performed as described above. Additionally, the anti-interference ability of the proposed method for morin detection was examined. Different anti-interference substances (0.2 mM) were separately added into the SNCDs solution. Subsequently, morin (0.075 mM) was introduced into the SNCDs mixture. The fluorescence intensities were accordingly measured. All experiments were performed in triplicate. To detect morin in real samples, 0.1 % BSA samples were prepared by dissolving BSA powder in PBS solution. The reliability of the developed sensing platform was evaluated using the relative standard deviation (RSD) values. The LOD was calculated by 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the fitted curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging in zebrafish for detecting morin
ce:section.ce:para: Wild zebrafish at 5 d post-fertilization were incubated with an E3 medium containing 60 μL of SNCDs at a concentration of 1 mg/mL for 4 h. The zebrafish were then washed three times with fresh E3 nutrition solution to remove any remaining SNCDs. Subsequently, the zebrafish were exposed to morin for 4 h. Images were acquired in the range of 550–660 nm using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany) at an excitation of 488 nm.

Soybean seeds of similar sizes were selected and washed three times with Milli-Q water to remove chemical residues. One hundred soybean seeds were germinated in a growth chamber for 3 d in the dark (temperature: 25℃, humidity: 50–60 %). Following germination, the seedlings were cultured in the hydroponic system using 50 % Hoagland's nutrient solution containing different concentrations of SNCDs (0, 0.5, 1.0, and 5.0 mg/L) for one week. Each treatment was replicated three times to ensure accuracy. After 7 d, soybean seedlings were harvested to measure growth parameters including root length, stem length, fresh weight, dry weight, and moisture level.

NADP+ and NADPH were extracted from the soybean seedlings using acidic and alkaline extraction solutions, respectively. NADPH was determined by the reduction of oxidized thiazole blue to formazan via the transfer of hydrogen from PMS. The NADP+ content was measured by reducing NADP+ to NADPH using glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase.

The total protein content of plants was analyzed using the Thomas Brilliant Blue method due to the binding of Thomas Brilliant Blue dye to the protein. Simultaneously, soluble sugars can be oxidized to furfural by concentrated sulfuric acid, which subsequently reacts with anthranone to produce blue-green furfural derivatives. Therefore, soluble sugar in the fresh leaves can be determined through colorimetry at 630 nm.

Since ATPase breaks down ATP to produce ADP and inorganic phosphorus, ATPase activities can be evaluated by analyzing the amount of inorganic phosphorus. Na+K+-ATPase, Ca2+Mg2+-ATPase, Ca2+-ATPase, and Mg2+-ATPase levels in fresh plant roots were measured according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

The SNCDs solution (5.0 mg/mL) was filled into an empty pen without any modifications to serve as anticounterfeiting fluorescence ink. For the PVA/SNCDs film, 10 % (m/m) PVA was prepared by stirring vigorously at 85℃ until complete dissolution. The PVA solution was then mixed with a certain amount of SNCDs solution. The resulting PVA/SNCDs solution was transferred to a clean Petri dish and dried at 45℃ for 12 h.

The data are presented as mean ± standard error (SE). Statistical significance was analyzed using a one-way analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA) followed by the Waller–Duncan comparison test, using IBM SPSS Statistics 22. When compared with the control group, the statistical significance threshold was set at *p<0.05, ** p< 0.01, *** p< 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and composition of SNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: SNCDs were dispersed in an aqueous solution with a red color and orange fluorescence under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination, respectively. TEM, FTIR, and XPS were performed to examine the morphology, structure, and composition of the as-prepared SNCDs. The TEM images reveal that the prepared SNCDs were spherical with an average size of 2.40 nm (Figs. 1b and 1c). Well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.17 nm can also be observed, closely resembling the (100) facet of graphite. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d), a broad absorption peak at 3259–3329 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrational band of O–H from the carboxylic group or N–H from the amine structure [24,30], indicating the presence of residual N–H groups on the surface of the SNCDs. The absorption peak at 2982 cm−1 can be attributed to C–H stretching vibration [18]. The peaks at 1239 and 1389 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–N and S–O, respectively [22,31], whereas the absorption band at 1058 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of SO/C–O [32,33]. Furthermore, the peak at 576 corresponds to C–S bond in the synthesized SNCDs [31].

These FTIR results were further validated by XPS analyses of the SNCDs (Figs. 1d–1h). The signals at 290.8, 399.8, 527.2, and 186.3 eV indicate the presence of C (66.39 %), N (10.07 %), O (19.86 %) and S (3.68 %). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum shows two peaks at 284.8 and 285.9 eV, which can be attributed to C–C/CC and C−O/C−N [26,34]. The N 1 s spectrum shows two peaks near 399.3 and 401.7 eV, which correspond to amino N and pyrrolic N [13], indicating that the two precursors underwent a certain degree of carbonization during the formation of SNCDs to produce numerous nitrogenous fused-ring structures. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum features two fitting peaks, which can be attributed to C–O (533.8 eV) and O–H (529.5 eV) [16]. The S 2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 167.8 and 169.9 eV for 2p2/3 and 2p1/2 C–S bands [31], confirming the successful preparation of SNCDs. These hydrophilic groups endow the SNCDs with good water solubility. Furthermore, the abundant N/O substructures on the surface impart excellent solubility to SNCDs in various solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties and sensing platform for morin
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of SNCDs were further investigated ( Fig. 2). The absorption peak at 246 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of SNCDs in water corresponds to the π–π* transition for CC of the carbon core, whereas the weak absorption peaks at 276 nm and 520 nm are assigned to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains and n–π* transition for CO and CS/CN, respectively. The optimal fluorescence emission peak of the SNCDs at a concentration of 1 mg/mL is centered at approximately 592 nm (FWHM, 78 nm) with an excitation wavelength of 527 nm. The bandgap energy was calculated to be 4.230 eV based on the Tauc plot (Fig. 2b) [35].

The fluorescence of CDs originates mainly from conjugated structure, surface state, and size [36]. The SNCDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior, which can likely be attributed to carbon core/surface defects rather than size effect [37]. Fig. 2c shows that the deconvoluted fluorescence spectra of the SNCDs have two separate wide peaks, with maximum emission peaks at 582 nm, and 623 nm, respectively, indicating the presence of two photoluminescence centers. Furthermore, the two lifetimes of 1.11 and 4.11 ns of the prepared SNCDs confirm two fluorescence centers (Fig. 2a: Inset), which stem from the core- and surface-state fluorescence [38,39], respectively. Further studies verify no noticeable variation in fluorescence intensity along with pH (3–11), KCl concentration, or irradiation time (Fig. 2d and Figs. S1 and S2). More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of SNCDs was maintained at a high level for 3 months (Fig. S3).

The prepared SNCDs were well dispersed in water, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethyl formamide (DMF), ethanol, and ethylenediamine (EDA), whereas the SNCDs solutions in ethyl acetate, acetone, and tetrahydrofuran (THF), were opaque and exhibited relatively poor solubility, confirming that the SNCDs are hydrophilic nanomaterials. Interestingly, the SNCDs in solvents of various polarities exhibited solvent-dependent optical features (Fig. 2e). Additionally, the emission peaks shifted to red as the solvent polarity increased (Fig. 2f). The emissions were much stronger in various solvents than in water, except for EDA and ethyl acetate. Previous studies have shown that the stable excited states of such structures can be influenced by the dipole moment of the solvent [40], thereby narrowing the energy gap. The solvatochromism phenomenon can be attributed to intramolecular charge transfer between solvent molecules or the presence of surface trap states [23], resulting in variations of energy gaps (29 nm) across different solvents. These experimental results suggest that the SNCDs are affected by solvent. The previous study has shown that chemical doping with heteroatoms such as P, B, N, and S is the efficient pathway to regulate the luminescence behavior [41]. The quantum yield of SNCDs was determined to be 7.80 % using cresol purple as a reference, which is slightly higher than that reported for pure safranine, which is attributed to the successful doping of N and S elements [41].

Since the complex composition of natural samples poses great challenges for fluorescence sensing platforms [42], the fluorescence emission of the SNCDs in the presence of other coexisting substances, including metal, antibiotics, and dyes was investigated. As depicted in Figs. 3a and 3b, none of these substances, except for morin, caused a noticeable fluorescence quenching response of the SNCDs, indicating excellent selectivity toward morin. An anti-interference assay was also conducted to assess the possible interference of various co-existing substances on morin detection (Fig. 3c). When morin and other several substances were added to the SNCDs solution, the fluorescence of SNCDs at 592 nm was still significantly quenched in the presence of the substances tested. This finding indicates that the prepared SNCDs exhibit a strong response to morin, and the other coexisting substances do not interfere with the ability of SNCDs to detect morin. The fluorescence intensity of the SNCDs decreased gradually with increasing morin (Figs. 3d–3e), indicating that the SNCDs-based sensing platform is effective and potential for morin detection. Furthermore, two linear relationships can be achieved between morin concentration and fluorescence quenching efficiency (Fig. 3f). The LOD was calculated to be 0.31 μM based on the 3σ/slope principle. The binding constant between SNCDs and morin was calculated to be 3.34×103 M−1 using the modified Benesi−Hildebrand plot (Fig. S4).

Upon the irradiation at 365 and 254 nm, the fluorescent SNCDs exhibited noticeable color changes from dark orange to pale orange as the morin concentration increased. A smartphone-assisted system can be established to detect the morin [43,44]. A series of solutions under the irradiation of 365 and 254 nm were captured using a smartphone camera and the output results were converted to RGB values using the ColorPicker application ( Figs. 4a, 4b, 4d, and 4e). The corresponding R/G values varied with the concentration of morin (Figs. 4c and 4f). Good linear relationships (R2=0.9927 and 0.9925) were achieved between R/G and morin concentration at 365 and 254 nm, respectively, with the LODs of 3.29 and 3.11 μM, respectively. Therefore, this convenient and easy-to-operated method is a potential tool for the quantitative detection of morin under on-site and outdoor conditions.

The construction of filter-based solid sensors can broaden the application scope of SNCDs. The SNCDs/filter composite material was prepared by soaking filter paper in an aqueous solution with SNCDs concentration of 1 mg/mL. The SNCDs, either in solution or embedded in filter paper, emitted orange-red/yellow fluorescence under 365 and 254 nm light ( Fig. 5a). The fluorescence of the fabricated SNCDs/filter strip sensor could be quenched by morin, but not by other ions, antibiotics or dyes (Fig. S5), implying its excellent selectivity. Alterations in the fluorescence color of the sensing platforms with different concentrations of morin were distinguishable by the naked eye. Two good linear relationships were obtained with LODs of 0.19 and 0.43 μM, respectively (Figs. 5b and 5c), which are much lower than the solid sensor reported in a previous study [45]. The fabricated solid sensor was superior to the aqueous solution and smartphone-assisted sensor.

Owing to the penetration of SNCDs into zebrafish, exceptional fluorescence in zebrafish was also observed (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, the zebrafish morphology did not change during the experiment. Following the incubation with morin, the fluorescence weakened after 4h-exposure, indicating that the SNCDs-based sensor can be applied to visually detect morin in vivo.

The results of previous studies are summarized in Table S1. Compared with these methods, the sensing platforms of SNCDs for morin have more applicability than the single-signal sensing platforms. The obtained LOD is better than some previously reported. Furthermore, the as-prepared SNCDs exhibited long-wavelength emission and allowed deep penetration into biological tissues.

To elucidate the binding mechanism of the SNCDs to morin, the fluorescence lifetime and absorption spectrum of the SNCDs after treatment with morin were determined. Fluorescence quenching typically results from static, dynamic, or combined quenching [36,46,47]. Dynamic quenching occurs through electron and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. No overlap can be observed between the absorption band of morin and the fluorescence spectra of the SNCDs ( Fig. 6a), ruling out the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the sensing platform. Additionally, UV–vis absorption spectra of the SNCDs in the absence and presence of morin were different (Fig. 6b), suggesting that a static quenching mechanism is involved in the interaction between the SNCDs with morin. The SNCDs and SNCDs/morin fluorescence delays are depicted in Fig. 6c. Notably, the average lifetime of SNCDs decreased with the successive addition of morin. The non-linear fitted curve (Fig. 6d) of F0-F/F versus morin concentration confirms that the quenching process results from a combination of mechanisms involving both dynamic and static quenching.

Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was evaluated using the Stern–Volmer equation [13]. Ksv value was determined to be 0.661 M−1 according to the Stern–Volmer plot. The shorter lifetime of SNCDs-morin mixture than that of pure SNCDs may be attributed to non-radiative relaxation [48]. Therefore, the static and dynamic quenching mechanisms are the predominant factors determining the quenching mechanism of the sensing platform.

Moreover, morin possesses quinone and phenol moieties, which can accept and donate electrons to other substances, respectively. Therefore, when morin and the SNCDs are close to each other, non-radioactive electron transfer is possibly proposed to be responsible for fluorescence quenching in the sensing platform [49]. The energy levels of the SNCDs were determined from electrochemical studies using the empirical Eq. (1) [48,50]. Fig. 6e exhibits a reduction peak (−0.644 V). The reduction potential was calculated to be 3.756 eV, which corresponds to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Therefore, the EHOMO of the SNCDs was obtained to be (3.756–4.230) eV=−0.474 eV. The energy levels of the LUMO and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)of morin were calculated to be −1.670 eV and −5.750 eV, respectively, through B3LYP(D3)6–31 G* dispersion correction for structural optimization using Gaussian16 W (Fig. 6f). These findings provide some evidence of the photo-induced electron transfer from SNCDs to morin.
(1) Eg= ELUMO− EHOMO

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of morin in bovine serum
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability, reliability, and accuracy of the fluorescence sensing platform in complicated samples were validated by spiking dilute bovine serum with a certain amount of morin using the standard addition method. The morin concentrations in spiked samples were found to be consistent with those in the added morin samples in the range of 0.00–55.00 μM ( Table 1). The recovery rates of the developed sensing platform range from 100.50 % to 103.81 % and the inter-day RSDs are between 0.29 % and 2.88 %. These findings demonstrate the satisfactory reliability and accuracy of the proposed sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of SNCDs on soybean growth
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthesis is a crucial process in plant growth. A prerequisite for light reaction is the light-driven transfer of electrons and protons within the thylakoid membrane [51]. Several reports have revealed that CDs from various precursors positively affect plant growth [31,52]. The prepared SNCDs exhibited strong absorption in the UV and visible regions and emitted orange fluorescence in aqueous solution, indicating that the SNCDs are similar to photosynthetic pigments, which have the potential to enhance plant photosynthetic efficiency [25]. To validate whether safranine-derived SNCDs affect the growth and development of plants, soybean seedlings were used as a model. After exposure to SNCDs for 7 d, the phenotypes of the soybean seedlings, including root length, stem length, fresh weight, and moisture level were recorded. The root length, stem length, and fresh weight of the seedlings significantly increased in the 0.5 and/or 1.0 mg/L SNCDs-treated groups, and then slightly decreased when the concentration was increased to 5.0 mg/mL ( Figs. 7a–7c). Based on the fresh and dry biomasses, the moisture levels of soybean seedlings were also determined (Fig. 7d and Fig. S6). The seedlings exposed to 0.5 and 1.0 mg/mL possess higher water content than the control. A significant increase in stem length in relation to that of the control is presumably caused by the presence of hydrophilic groups such as COOH, –OH, and –NH2 on the SNCDs surface, which offer additional binding sites for water molecules to allow penetration into the plant. Consequently, these additional binding sites increase the moisture content and then facilitate the growth of soybean seedlings [21]. The SNCDs can enhance the water availability of soybean seedlings and have a favorable effect on plant growth.

To examine the effects of the SNCDs on the biochemical indices of the plants, the sugar and total protein contents of the seedlings were assessed (Figs. 7e and 7 f). The soybean seedlings treated with 0.5 mg/L SNCDs exhibited a decreasing trend in total protein and soluble sugar contents, whereas those treated with 1.0 mg/L SNCDs showed an increasing trend. In contrast, the soybean seedlings treated with 5 mg/L SNCDs exhibited a significant decrease in soluble sugar and total protein contents compared with the control. These findings indicate that protein and sugar levels depend on the concentration of SNCDs.

During photosynthesis, NADPH and NADP+ play crucial important roles in light reaction and carbon assimilation [22]. In this study, NADPH and NADP+ contents increased simultaneously in all the groups, reaching their maximum values in the 0.5 mg/L SNCDs group, which can be attributed to the photoinduced electron-donating and accepting properties that accelerate water splitting [21]. Such accelerated electron transfer favors the conversion of NADPH to NADP+, consequently promoting ATP synthesis [13]. However, electron production is a double-edged sword; excess electrons can lead to the generation of reactive oxygen species [22], ultimately inhibiting the growth of soybean seedlings (Figs. 7g–7 l). Notably, SNCDs enhance electron and proton transfer and promote plant growth to a certain extent.

Following the light reaction, CO2 is converted to carbohydrates, proteins, and sugars. Furthermore, the metal ion content of plants is crucial for plant growth, with ionic balance serving as an important mechanism of plant salt tolerance [25]. The metal ions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+, in plant cells are known to play key roles in regulating various physiological functions within plant cells, including the transport of sodium, potassium, and calcium to maintain their balance inside the cell. Almost all cell membranes contain ATPases. A significant increase in the concentration of membrane transport enzymes (Na+K+-ATPase, Ca2+-ATPase, Ca2+Mg2+-ATPase, and Mg2+-ATPase) of soybean seedlings exposed to the highest dose of SNCDs reflects their strong ability to regulate intracellular Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ levels. Variation in these ATPase suggests a strong interaction between Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and SNCDs, which affects soybean seedlings growth. However, in agreement with previous studies [21,53], high-concentration SNCDs do not exhibit beneficial effects in promoting plant growth, probably due to membrane damage. Further studies are necessary to elucidate the mechanisms underlying soybean growth regulated by the SNCDs.

These data suggest that SNCDs can effectively accelerate water splitting and increase the efficiency of the light reaction in photosynthesis in soybean plants. The use of low-concentration SNCDs promoted the growth of soybean seedling, whereas high-concentration SNCDs induced membrane injury, thereby inhibiting plant yields.

In practice, living environments are complex and variable. Therefore, advanced anticounterfeiting materials that can utilize the response of SNCDs under various conditions are required. As shown in Fig. 2, the average lifetime is extremely short, which is conducive to a fast response to anticounterfeiting. Therefore, we applied the as-prepared SNCDs as a “writing ink”. When written on SNCDs-soaked and untreated filters, the number patterns emitted dark orange-red fluorescence under daylight and irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S7a). Upon excitation at 254 nm, orange-yellow fluorescence was observed. This suggests that the prepared SNCDs can be used as fluorescent ink in anticounterfeiting applications. Moreover, the SNCDs can be uniformly dispersed not only in aqueous solution for applications as fluorescent inks but also in PVA to prepare flexible and hard solid-state films. As shown in Fig. S7b, PVA/SNCD films were almost transparent and colorless in daylight. In contrast, they emitted yellow fluorescence with concentration-dependent behavior upon 254 nm excitation, thus expanding the application scope of SNCDs in the solid state.

Orange SNCDs-based visual and optical sensing platforms were fabricated to detect morin in aqueous solutions and filter matrices. Compared with single-output signal, the sensing platforms offer multiple signals for morin and provide mutual verification of the results, thereby, improving the accuracy and reliability of detection. Furthermore, the SNCDs could promote plant growth owing to their broad absorption and emission regions, which are pivotal in light reaction process. Notably, the as-prepared SNCDs can be used as fluorescence ink/film in anticounterfeiting applications.

Chuan Dong: Supervision, Resources. Xintong Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Zhenhua Yang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yuexia Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xianyun Zheng: Funding acquisition. Quanxi Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2021–014 and 2023–030) and the Applied Basic Research Programs of the Science and Technology Department of Shanxi Province (20210302123472).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.114244.
